title: Simplest Wood Chaise Lounge
summary: A reclining outdoor lounge chair with a pivot-bolted backrest that adjusts against a stop block — a genuinely mechanical outdoor chair, not just a fixed-angle lounger.
description:
This chaise's whole identity is its reclining back — pivoted on a bolt through the main frame rather than fixed at one angle, so the backrest genuinely adjusts and rests against a stop block when fully reclined. Build the main frame and the back frame first, notching the main frame for the back support with a jigsaw — **2x6 lumber is used for both frames** rather than the thinner 2x4 shown in some older diagrams of this design, a deliberate strengthening update carried over from user feedback on the original build.

Add the seat slats to the main frame, then attach the legs in two rows — the first row supports the main frame, and the second, taller row up front is what gives the chaise its slight recline angle and, not incidentally, provides a resting spot for the stop block when the back is folded fully down.

**The pivot bolt connection is the one step worth doing with the pieces clamped in place rather than pre-drilled separately** — clamp the back frame against the main frame at the desired pivot point, then drill through both simultaneously so the holes line up exactly; drilling them independently risks a bolt that doesn't move freely once assembled. Attach the back slats to the back frame either before or after this pivot connection is made — user feedback on this design notes it's genuinely easier to bolt the frames together first and add the back slats afterward.

tools.note: Drilling the pivot bolt holes through both frames simultaneously.

materials.name: 1/4" bolts, 4 in, with nuts and washers
Exterior screws, 2", or galvanized brad nails and glue
Primer and paint, or exterior stain

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the two 9 ft 2x6s, cut two main frame sides 85" and two main frame front and back pieces 21" — one long and one short piece per board. From the three 8 ft 2x4s, cut six tall legs 14", two short legs 7", two back frame sides 32", three back frame rails 17 1/2" and the stop block 24". From the eight 8 ft 1x3s, cut sixteen seat slats 24" and seven back slats 37 1/2"; one back slat plus two seat slats fits an 8 ft board with room to spare. The 21" pieces between the 1 1/2" sides make the main frame 24" wide overall, which is exactly the seat slat length — so if a seat slat runs long during assembly, the frame is out of square, not the slat. Hold off cutting the back slats to 37 1/2": that length is longer than the back frame in either direction, so cut them to the frame you actually build.

steps.title: Build the main frame
steps.body: Join the two 85" sides to the 21" front and back pieces to make a box 24" wide and 85" long, using 1-1/2" pocket holes with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, or 2-3/4" self-tapping screws driven through the sides into the end grain. Measure both diagonals and correct until they match, because every slat, every leg and the pivot all register off this frame. Then notch the sides with the jigsaw where the back frame will drop in. The plan gives no dimensions for that notch, so build the back frame first if you prefer, hold it against the assembled main frame, mark the notch directly from the part, and cut to the line.

steps.title: Add the seat slats
steps.body: Lay the sixteen 24" slats across the main frame with their ends flush to the frame's outside faces. Sixteen slats at 2 1/2" wide is 40" of solid seat on an 85" frame, so they will not cover it end to end — space them evenly with a spacer block and stop them clear of the notched area where the back frame swings, or the back will not lie down. Fasten with 2-3/4" screws, or pocket-screw them from underneath for a clean top with no visible fasteners. Pre-drill near every slat end; 1x3 splits easily there.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: There are six 14" legs and two 7" legs. Attach the first row under the frame, then the second, taller row toward the front — that height difference is what gives the chaise its recline, and the taller front row is also what the stop block bears against when the back is folded flat. Which legs go in which row is not stated, so stand the frame on a flat floor with the legs held in place and confirm the recline reads right before you fasten anything. Use exterior glue at every joint and at least two or three 2-3/4" self-tapping screws per leg: a lounge chair puts its whole load through these connections.

steps.title: Build the back frame
steps.body: Build the back frame the same way as the main frame: the two 32" sides with the three 17 1/2" rails between them, one at each end and one in the middle, which makes a frame about 20 1/2" wide. Square it by the diagonals before the glue sets. Test-fit it into the notches in the main frame before going any further — a back frame even slightly too wide will bind against the sides instead of swinging freely on the pivot.

steps.title: Drill and install the pivot bolt
steps.body: Clamp the back frame in position against the main frame at the pivot point you want, held at the angle it will sit, then drill the 1/4" hole through both parts in a single pass so the holes cannot be out of line — drilling each part separately is how you end up with a bolt that binds. Fit the 4" bolts with a washer under the head and under the nut, and tighten only until the joint is snug; a pivot cranked down tight will not move. Swing the back through its full travel before you do anything else.
materials: 1/4" bolts, 4 in, with nuts and washers

steps.title: Attach the back slats
steps.body: Fasten the back slats across the back frame with 2" exterior screws, or galvanized brads and glue, spacing them with a block rather than by eye. It is genuinely easier after the pivot bolts are in, since the frame is held at a workable angle instead of moving around loose. Cut each slat to the width of the frame you actually built — the 37 1/2" listed length is longer than the back frame in either direction, so measure across the assembled frame and cut to that.
materials: Exterior screws, 2", or galvanized brad nails and glue

steps.title: Add the stop block
steps.body: Fasten the 24" 2x4 stop block across the main frame where it catches the back frame at full recline: set the back to the flattest angle you want, mark where the frame lands, and fasten there with 2-3/4" self-tapping screws and exterior glue. Its 24" length matches the frame's outside width, so it also ties the two sides together. Cycle the back through its whole range with weight on it before you finish, because moving the block later means filling screw holes in a visible face.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand everything and round over every edge and corner — this is a chair for bare skin. Then apply primer and paint, or an exterior stain, coating the undersides and the end grain of every slat as well; end grain is where an outdoor chair takes on water and starts to fail. Re-check the pivot bolts once the finish has cured, and again after the first season outdoors.
materials: Primer and paint, or exterior stain

images.alt: Reclining outdoor chaise lounge with a pivoting backrest
